title: The Third Sunday after Pentecost
context:
# TEMPORAL CONTEXT SNAPSHOT
Saint John's Episcopal Cathedral, Denver, Colorado
Sermon Date: June 21, 2020 | Preacher: Katie Pearson

## 1. LITURGICAL CONTEXT

The Third Sunday after Pentecost falls on June 21, 2020, placing the liturgy in Ordinary Time following the Pentecost season. The Revised Common Lectionary readings for this Sunday are likely 1 Samuel 17:1a, 4-11, 19-23, 32-40 (David and Goliath), Psalm 9:9-20, 2 Corinthians 6:1-13, and Mark 4:35-41 (Jesus calms the storm). The Gospel's theme of Jesus bringing peace amid chaos would have resonated with contemporary circumstances on this particular Sunday.

## 2. EPISCOPAL / ANGLICAN CHURCH NEWS

The Episcopal Church had recently (June 12, 2020) begun formal discussions following the removal of Robert E. Lee statues from Washington National Cathedral, representing significant denominational reckoning with Confederate monuments and institutional racism. The Diocese of Colorado was navigating reopening guidelines following COVID-19 lockdowns, with many congregations still operating under capacity restrictions or hybrid worship models. No major General Convention or bishop-election activities were occurring at this specific date, though national church leadership was actively responding to the George Floyd protests that had erupted nationally.

## 3. DENVER & COLORADO NEWS

Denver was in the midst of intense racial justice demonstrations following George Floyd's death two weeks prior, with downtown Denver experiencing nightly protests near the State Capitol and active community organizing around police reform. The city had experienced significant civil unrest June 1-7, with vandalism, fires, and heavy police response, though the weekend of June 20-21 was calmer following community-led de-escalation efforts and dialogue between activists and city officials. Weather was typical early summer for Denver: clear and warm, with highs in the low 80s Fahrenheit.

## 4. NATIONAL NEWS

The dominant national story was the ongoing George Floyd protests, now three weeks old, with the movement for racial justice and police reform at peak visibility, including calls to defund police departments and reckoning with systemic racism. The Trump administration was facing criticism for its handling of the protests, including the controversial June 1 clearing of Lafayette Square; the National Guard remained deployed in multiple cities. President Trump's campaign was ramping up for the 2020 election, with rallies resuming in Tulsa, Oklahoma (scheduled for June 20, though postponed to June 21 due to Juneteenth recognition). COVID-19 cases were rising in multiple states as economies reopened, with Texas and Florida emerging as hotspots.

## 5. INTERNATIONAL NEWS

Brazil was experiencing a major surge in COVID-19 cases, becoming the second-hardest hit country globally behind the United States, with deaths accelerating throughout June. The United Kingdom was beginning to ease COVID-19 lockdown restrictions, with non-essential shops reopening and outdoor gatherings permitted in larger groups. Hong Kong's National Security Law was moving toward final passage in the Chinese National People's Congress, threatening the "one country, two systems" autonomy that had defined the territory and sparking international condemnation.

## 6. CULTURAL MOMENT

The nation was experiencing profound reckoning with systemic racism and police brutality, with a notable generational and geographic breadth to the George Floyd protests; conversations about Confederate monuments, institutional racism, and social justice dominated media, churches, and community spaces. The cultural moment was one of genuine crisis and upheaval mixed with widespread participation in activism, creating both urgency and exhaustion in many communities. Sports were tentatively returning after COVID-19 disruptions, with the NFL and NBA preparing for modified seasons; the Tokyo Olympics remained postponed, creating uncertainty in the sports calendar. The summer of 2020 would come to be characterized as a pivotal moment of racial reckoning in American institutions, including churches.
